message.content: Adversaries may exploit the task scheduling functionality in operating systems to execute malicious code either initially or on a recurring basis. This technique, known as "Scheduled Task or Job," leverages built-in utilities to schedule programs or scripts at specified times. It can be used for persistence by executing programs at system startup or on a scheduled basis, often under elevated permissions. Remote task scheduling requires proper authentication and typically administrative privileges. Common exploitation methods include using commands like `schtasks` in Windows or similar utilities in other OSes. Notable examples include Earth Lusca using `schtasks` for persistence, Lokibot setting timers for execution, and Remsec creating new scheduled tasks. Mitigation strategies involve monitoring and restricting the creation of scheduled tasks, employing least privilege principles, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect abnormal task scheduling activities. Regular audits of scheduled tasks can also help identify unauthorized or suspicious entries.
